steps.title: The Drawing Endgame
steps.text:
## Opposite-coloured bishops in the endgame

When each side has a single bishop and they travel on **different colours**, the resulting endgame is famously **drawish**. The reason is geometric: the two bishops can never directly oppose each other, and the defender can build an unbreakable **fortress** on the colour his bishop controls.

### What this endgame looks like

- One bishop per side, **opposite colours** (one light, one dark).
- King and pawn count usually equal or close.
- Even with **two extra pawns**, the stronger side often cannot make progress.

### The key concept: the defender's colour

The defender:

- Places his **bishop and king on the colour** his bishop controls.
- **Blockades** every passed pawn on a square of his bishop's colour.
- Refuses to push pawns — pawn moves create new colour weaknesses.

The attacker has no way to remove the bishop, no way to breakthrough the colour wall.

### Procedure for the stronger side (when winning is possible)

1. Look for **two passed pawns separated by 2+ files** — too far apart for one bishop to stop both.
2. Use the **king actively** as the third attacker.
3. Aim for **pawn breakthroughs on the attacker's bishop's colour**.
4. Avoid trading the **rook or queen** if you have one alongside — see the next page for why.

### Procedure for the defender

1. Place the king and bishop on the **right colour**.
2. **Blockade** the most advanced enemy pawn.
3. Move your bishop only along its diagonal — never trade pieces.
4. **Wait**. The half point usually arrives by itself.

### Common mistakes

- The stronger side **trading queens or rooks** into a pure opposite-bishop ending — many won positions become draws.
- The defender **pushing pawns** unnecessarily — every push opens a new weak square.
- Forgetting the **two-pawn rule**: even +2 is not enough if the pawns are connected and on the bishop's colour.

### Model line (schematic)

With **+2 pawns** on the same wing and connected, Black plays Be4-c2-d1 forever and White can never break the blockade.

> Opposite bishops in the endgame: half a point waiting to be claimed.

steps.title: Exception: With Queens
steps.text:
## The middlegame paradox

Here is one of chess's most beautiful inversions: in the **endgame**, opposite-coloured bishops favour the **defender**. In the **middlegame** — especially with **queens or rooks** still on the board — they favour the **attacker**.

### Why the inversion happens

If you launch an attack on the dark squares, the defender's **light-squared bishop** is *useless* for stopping it. He effectively defends with **one bishop fewer**. The attacker enjoys a permanent **piece-up feeling** in his attacking zone.

### Two visual examples in prose

- A White light-squared bishop on **b1** points at **h7**. Black has only a dark-squared bishop on g7. Black has **no defender** on the light squares around his king. White plays **Qd3** and the threat of mate on h7 is decisive.
- White's dark-squared bishop on **a3** rakes the long diagonal toward Black's king on g8 with pawns on **g7** and **h7**. Black's light bishop on g6 cannot oppose — the dark squares around the king are an invasion lane.

### How to USE the imbalance

1. **Identify which colour your bishop attacks** in the middlegame.
2. **Push pawns on the OPPOSITE colour** to fix targets your bishop can hit.
3. **Bring the queen** along your bishop's diagonal — they coordinate perfectly.
4. **Avoid trades** that would leave a pure opposite-bishop endgame — keep the heavy pieces.

### What signs tell you to apply it

- Your bishop and queen agree on a colour, the opponent's bishop disagrees.
- The enemy king's shelter has weaknesses on **your** colour.
- The position has **pieces still on the board** — opposite bishops only attack while there is firepower around them.

### Common mistakes

- **Trading queens** in an attack — the imbalance evaporates with the heavy pieces.
- Pushing pawns on **your bishop's colour** — they block your own diagonals.
- Believing "opposite bishops draw" applies in every phase. With queens, opposite bishops *win*.

> Same bishops draw, opposite bishops in the endgame draw — but opposite bishops with queens attack.
